title: Direct observation of the transverse near field of an edge excitation and associated slow secondary dynamics in a fractional quantum Hall state
authors: Yunhyeon Jeong, Akinori Kamiyama, John N. Moore, Takaaki Mano, Ken-ichi Sasaki, Yuuki Sugiyama, Tokiro Numasawa, Masahiro Hotta, Go Yusa
abstract: We report stroboscopic time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) microscopy and spectroscopy revealing the transverse near field of an edge excitation in a $ν=1/3$ fractional quantum Hall (FQH) state. Time-resolved $y$-$t$ maps reveal an immediate PL response extending more than $30~μ\mathrm{m}$ into the bulk transverse to the edge when the edge magnetoplasmon (EMP) passes the mesa boundary. The nearly instantaneous nature of this long-range response identifies it as the non-radiative, quasi-electrostatic near field, revealing the EMP as a spatially extended collective excitation rather than a strictly one-dimensional charge-density oscillation. We also observe secondary bulk-side responses distinct from the immediate transverse near-field response. The coexistence of these immediate and secondary responses shows that electrically launched edge excitations produce bulk-side dynamics on widely separated time scales.
summary: This paper uses time-resolved microscopy to observe the electromagnetic field of an edge excitation in a fractional quantum Hall state. It demonstrates that these excitations are not purely 1D but include a spatially extended, quasi-electrostatic near-field that reaches deep into the bulk. This reveals that edge-driven dynamics can trigger complex, multi-scale responses in the adjacent electronic fluid.
main_problem: To determine the spatial and dynamical structure of edge excitations in fractional quantum Hall states, specifically whether edge magnetoplasmons are strictly 1D charge-density oscillations or involve spatially extended electromagnetic fields.
main_result: The study reveals that edge magnetoplasmons possess a long-range, nearly instantaneous transverse near-field extending over 30 micrometers into the bulk, alongside slower secondary bulk-side dynamics.
method: Stroboscopic time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) microscopy and spectroscopy using a Ti:sapphire laser synchronized with electrically launched voltage pulses.
model_or_system: A nu=1/3 fractional quantum Hall state in a 15-nm GaAs/AlGaAs quantum well at high magnetic field (14 T) and ultra-low temperatures (40-50 mK).
key_observables: Trion photoluminescence spectra (singlet and triplet peaks), triplet PL intensity maps, peak energy shifts, and amplitude ratios.
important_parameters: Filling factor nu=1/3, magnetic field 14 T, temperature 40-50 mK, voltage pulse amplitude +/- 50 to 100 mV, and repetition periods of 13 ns and 52 ns.
main_assumptions: The linewidth parameter is assumed to be common to both singlet and triplet trion components to stabilize the spectral fitting.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental schematic; Fig 2 displays PL spectra snapshots showing downstream spectral changes; Fig 3 shows spatial maps of PL intensity and reflectance; Fig 4 presents color maps of triplet peak energy shifts and amplitude ratios as functions of position and time.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental setup and device geometry, presents the time-resolved spectroscopic results showing the transverse near-field, analyzes the temporal evolution and secondary dynamics, and concludes by discussing the implications for the nature of edge excitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Optical microscope image of the measurement device. The excitation gate was driven by a voltage-pulse source (see SM for the details). The dashed rectangle labeled “Scan area” corresponds to Fig. 3. Lines A, B, and C indicate the measurement paths for Figs. 2 and 4. Grounded electrodes are part of coplanar waveguide structures [27].

FIG. 2. (a) and (b) Micro-PL spectra measured as snapshots at po- sitions along the 𝑦direction, perpendicular to the mesa boundary defined by 𝑦= 0: 𝑦= 1, 8, 18, 28, and 33 𝜇m. The delay time is 𝑡= −3.0 ns for both panels (see SM for more details). (a) Spec- tra measured along Line A, 16 𝜇m upstream from the center of the excitation gate. (b) Spectra measured along Line B, 16 𝜇m down- stream from the center of the excitation gate. The dotted curves show spectra measured without the excitation pulse. Insets in (a) and (b) schematically indicate the measurement locations (Lines A and B), as defined in Fig. 1. The pulse amplitude is ±100 mV for both pan- els. The spectra shown in (b) were...

FIG. 4. Triplet PL peak-energy shift [(a), (c)] and amplitude ratio [(b), (d)], shown as color maps as functions of 𝑦and 𝑡. Panels (a) and (b) were measured along Line C, ∼42 𝜇m downstream of the excitation gate, with 𝑇rep ∼52 ns; panels (c) and (d) were measured along Line B, 16 𝜇m downstream, with 𝑇rep ∼13 ns. The absolute origin of 𝑡is defined separately for the Line-C and Line-B measurements; see the SM for details. The voltage-pulse configuration and biasing conditions are described in the SM.

FIG. 3. 51×50 𝜇m2 spatial maps measured in the region indicated as the scan area in Fig. 1. (a) Reflectance map acquired with the same optical setup at 40–50 mK; the yellow region indicates the excitation gate. (b)–(d) Triplet PL intensity maps of the same region measured as snapshots at 𝑡= −3.0 ns with an exposure time of 10 s. Here, the triplet PL intensity was obtained by integrating the PL spectrum over 1533.45–1534.70 meV, without spectral fitting. (b) Triplet PL intensity map recorded with no voltage pulse applied to the excitation gate. (c) and (d) Maps obtained with a bipolar voltage pulse applied to the excitation gate.
